Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients  | Oil and fat

**Margarine** or **oleomargarine** is a synthetic butter substitute used in cooking and baking.[[1]](#cite_note-:8-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

At its core, margarine is an emulsion of water in solid fat, combined with added coloring, flavoring, preservatives, and/or emulsifiers to mimic the properties of butter.[[3]](#cite_note-:11-3) The fat component may derive from animal products or vegetable products;[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) if the fat used is liquid at room temperature, it will need to be hydrogenated for solidity.[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8) Generally, margarine has the same base proportions as butter, with about 80–85% fat and 16–18% water,[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:2-9)[[3]](#cite_note-:11-3) often with salt and milk solids for flavor,[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[9]](#cite_note-:2-9)[[3]](#cite_note-:11-3) plus colorant to make it pale yellow.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) Still, however, the flavor and texture do not match those of butter.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8) Low-fat margarines have a higher proportion of water and increased stabilizers to compensate.[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8)[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10) The melting range of margarine is similar to that of butter, at 94–98°F (34–36°C), but it does not melt quite as fast as the latter.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8) This gives it a slightly greasier mouthfeel than butter's.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8) Some margarines are plastic and spreadable at fridge temperatures,[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) which can be helpful in some baking applications.[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8)[[11]](#cite_note-:7-11)

### Varieties

[edit | edit source]

Because margarine is a "designer" synthetic fat product, a number of different formulations with different properties are available for different purposes.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[9]](#cite_note-:2-9)[[11]](#cite_note-:7-11) **Table margarine** is primarily meant to be soft and spreadable, melting fully in the mouth.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) These may be sold in tubs or sticks/blocks,[[3]](#cite_note-:11-3) with tub margarine being especially soft.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:6-8) Slightly sturdier **baker's or cake margarine** works well for creaming and can be used to make frostings whose texture will be stable.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) Its melting point is around 95°–110°F (35°–41°C).[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Even firmer and slightly waxier is **roll-in or pastry margarine**, which has a higher melting point (105–115°F / 41–46°C) and works well for laminated pastries.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[9]](#cite_note-:2-9) The firmest with the highest final melting point (115–135°F / 47–57°C) is **puff pastry margarine**, which creates very flaky pastries but has a waxy texture unpleasant to some.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[9]](#cite_note-:2-9)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Make sure to select the variety of margarine most suitable for your application. Margarine with sufficient salt and preservatives can be kept long-term without refrigeration.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Otherwise, it needs to be stored in the fridge. Make sure to keep it away from light and moisture for the longest shelf life.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Margarine has a couple of applications, primarily as a substitute for butter when the latter cannot be used. For example, many (but not all) margarines are suitable for individuals adhering to restricted diets, such as vegans/vegetarians, those with allergies, etc.[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10) Specialty margarines designed for baking also tend to be tailored for specific structural attributes, such as greater plasticity when cold.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) Butter and margarine may be combined in a given recipe, but they must be the same consistency when mixed to ensure even blending.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

Generally, it's okay to substitute margarine and butter for each other,[[9]](#cite_note-:2-9) though the end result will be slightly different. In cases where butter flavor is paramount, it's best to use it instead of margarine (dietary restrictions aside).*[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)* Reduced-fat margarines cannot be used in high-heat cooking applications, since their stabilizer content causes them to scorch and fail to melt correctly.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) They also cannot usually be used in baking due to their skewed proportions.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)
